perturbation.original_text: Within 7 days of a written request by the Discloser, the Recipient must return all Confidential Information, and provide a signed certificate stating that al l Confidential Information in the Recipient's possession or control has been returned.
perturbation.changed_text: Within 7 days of a written request by the Discloser, the Recipient should endeavor to return all Confidential Information, but is not required to provide a signed certificate.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'must return' to 'should endeavor' introduces ambiguity regarding the obligation to return confidential information. The removal of the requirement for a signed certificate further weakens the obligation and contradicts the strict confidentiality requirements outlined earlier in the document. This introduces an internal contradiction if other sections assume the return of information is mandatory and verifiable. For instance, clause 4 states 'maintained confidential with at least the standard of care that the Recipient maintains t o protect its own confidential information;' implying more than just endeavoring to return information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION OBLIGATIONS In consideration of each party disclosing its Confidential Information to the other party for the Purpose, the Recipient must ensure that Confidential Information is:  maintained confidential with at least the standard of care that the Recipient maintains t o protect its own confidential information;  not disclosed to any Unauthorised Person;  only used for the Purpose; and  not reproduced in any form except as required for the purpose. The Recipient must ensure that access to Confidential Information is only given to those of its officers, employees and advisers who require access for the Purpose, and that those officers, employees and advisers are informed of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information an d kee p that information confidential. Parties to whom the information is imparted must be warned that include d ‘insider information ,’ regarding publicly listed and traded shares may preclud e them from such trade in those shares . The Confidential Informatio n remains at all times the property of the Discloser, and other than as provided in this agreement, no rights in respect of the Confidential Information are granted or conveyed to the Recipient. Confidentiality & Non -Disclosure Agreement ICT Strategic Consulting 2 of 2 Ph: +61 (02) 9591 1464 56 Minnamorra Avenue Earlwood NSW 2206 Australia www.ICTStrategicConsulting.com In the event that the Recipient is legally required to disclo se any Confidential Information, the Recipient must immediately notify the Discloser of that fact. Within 7 days of a written request by the Discloser, the Recipient should endeavor to return all Confidential Information, but is not required to provide a signed certificate stating that al l Confidential Information in the Recipient's possession or control has been returned.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'must return' with 'should endeavor to return' and removes the requirement for a signed certificate. This contradicts the 'CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION OBLIGATIONS' section, which states that 'the Recipient must ensure that Confidential Information is maintained confidential with at least the standard of care that the Recipient maintains to protect its own confidential information'. The 'should endeavor' language weakens the obligation to return information, making it inconsistent with the mandatory language of the contradicted text, and creates ambiguity regarding the recipient's responsibility and introducing a contradiction. The absence of a required certificate of return further weakens the ability to ensure compliance with confidentiality obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The parties make no warranties or representations to each other in relation to any Confidential Information including (without limitation) as to the accur acy or completeness of any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties make no warranties or representations to each other in relation to any Confidential Information including (without limitation) as to the accur acy of any Confidential Information but do warrant that the information is provided in good faith and believed to be substantially complete.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that there are no warranties for accuracy but warranting good faith and substantial completeness introduces a contradiction. 'Good faith' implies a level of accuracy and reliability, creating ambiguity. This is in contradiction with the earlier clause 3, which defines Confidential Information without any qualifiers about accuracy. If the information is later found to be incomplete, the 'good faith' warranty is hard to define against the 'no warranties' clause, creating an In-text contradiction, specifically to Clause 3 where the definitions are.
perturbation.contradicted_text: DEFINITIONS AND INTERPRETATIONS "Confidential Information " of a disclosing party (" Discloser ") means the following , regardless of its form and including copies made by the receiving party (" Recipient "), whether the Recipient becomes aware of it before or after the date of this Agreement:  all information treated by the Discloser as confidential; and  disclosed by the Discloser to the Recipient or o f which the Recipient becomes aware, including but not limited to, the information specified in Schedule 1; except where that information is:  made public other than by the Reci pient breaching this agreement ;  legally required to be disclose d by the Recipie nt;  known to the Recipient free of any obligation to keep it confidential; or  Independently developed by the Recipient without use, directly or indirectly of Confidential Information received from the Discloser. "Unauthorised Persons " means officers, empl oyees or advisers of the Recipient who are not required to have access to the Confidential Information for the Purpose, and any other person who is not a party to this agreement. References to clauses and parties are references to clauses of, and parties t o, this agreement; references to things include parts of those things; references to persons include legal and natural persons; and words importing the singular include the plural and vice versa.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a warranty of 'good faith and believed to be substantially complete' regarding confidential information, which directly contradicts the original clause that explicitly states 'no warranties or representations...as to the accuracy' of confidential information. This directly contradicts the definition of Confidential Information in the Contradicted Text, creating an in-text contradiction. The original text does not provide any qualifiers about accuracy of the definition of Confidential Information, but the Changed Text does, making the original and changed text have a strong, direct conflict in terms. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' because the modified text creates uncertainty regarding the parties' obligations related to the accuracy and completeness of confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations in this agreement survive the termination or purported termination of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations in this agreement survive the termination or purported termination of this agreement, unless terminated due to a material breach by the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the condition that obligations don't survive termination if there's a material breach by the Discloser creates a contradiction. The original text states obligations survive *any* termination, but now there's an exception. Clause 4, regarding the maintenance of confidentiality, becomes unclear: does the Recipient still need to maintain confidentiality if the agreement was terminated due to the Discloser's breach? This added exception to clause 6 directly impacts the obligations described in clause 4, where it becomes an In-Text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION OBLIGATIONS In consideration of each party disclosing its Confidential Information to the other party for the Purpose, the Recipient must ensure that Confidential Information is:  maintained confidential with at least the standard of care that the Recipient maintains t o protect its own confidential information;  not disclosed to any Unauthorised Person;  only used for the Purpose; and  not reproduced in any form except as required for the purpose. The Recipient must ensure that access to Confidential Information is only given to those of its officers, employees and advisers who require access for the Purpose, and that those officers, employees and advisers are informed of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information an d kee p that information confidential. Parties to whom the information is imparted must be warned that include d ‘insider information ,’ regarding publicly listed and traded shares may preclud e them from such trade in those shares . The Confidential Informatio n remains at all times the property of the Discloser, and other than as provided in this agreement, no rights in respect of the Confidential Information are granted or conveyed to the Recipient. Confidentiality & Non -Disclosure Agreement ICT Strategic Consulting 2 of 2 Ph: +61 (02) 9591 1464 56 Minnamorra Avenue Earlwood NSW 2206 Australia www.ICTStrategicConsulting.com In the event that the Recipient is legally required to disclo se any Confidential Information, the Recipient must immediately notify the Discloser of that fact. Within 7 days of a written request by the Discloser, the Recipient must return all Confidential Information, and provide a signed certificate stating that al l Confidential Information in the Recipient's possession or control has been returned.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds an exception to the survival of obligations upon termination, stating that obligations do *not* survive termination if it is due to a material breach by the Discloser. The original text stated that all obligations survive *any* termination. This directly contradicts the Confidential Information Obligations section, which outlines the Recipient's continuous duties to maintain confidentiality and return information. The added exception creates ambiguity about whether these confidentiality obligations still apply if the termination resulted from the Discloser's breach, creating a strong and direct conflict and impacting the clarity of enforcement.

perturbation.original_text: maintained confidential with at least the standard of care that the Recipient maintains t o protect its own confidential information;
perturbation.changed_text: maintained confidential with no less care than a reasonable person would exercise under similar circumstances;
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates the recipient to use at least the same level of care they use to protect their own confidential information. The modification replaces this standard with 'no less care than a reasonable person'. This introduces ambiguity because a 'reasonable person' standard can be interpreted differently and might be a lower standard than how the Recipient actually protects their own highly sensitive information. Since this is in direct conflict with the clause's initial intent, a breach-of-contract case now has two interpretations which can lead to conflict in clause 4.
perturbation.contradicted_text: CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION OBLIGATIONS In consideration of each party disclosing its Confidential Information to the other party for the Purpose, the Recipient must ensure that Confidential Information is:  maintained confidential with no less care than a reasonable person would exercise under similar circumstances;  not disclosed to any Unauthorised Person;  only used for the Purpose; and  not reproduced in any form except as required for the purpose. The Recipient must ensure that access to Confidential Information is only given to those of its officers, employees and advisers who require access for the Purpose, and that those officers, employees and advisers are informed of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information an d kee p that information confidential. Parties to whom the information is imparted must be warned that include d ‘insider information ,’ regarding publicly listed and traded shares may preclud e them from such trade in those shares . The Confidential Informatio n remains at all times the property of the Discloser, and other than as provided in this agreement, no rights in respect of the Confidential Information are granted or conveyed to the Recipient. Confidentiality & Non -Disclosure Agreement ICT Strategic Consulting 2 of 2 Ph: +61 (02) 9591 1464 56 Minnamorra Avenue Earlwood NSW 2206 Australia www.ICTStrategicConsulting.com In the event that the Recipient is legally required to disclo se any Confidential Information, the Recipient must immediately notify the Discloser of that fact. Within 7 days of a written request by the Discloser, the Recipient must return all Confidential Information, and provide a signed certificate stating that al l Confidential Information in the Recipient's possession or control has been returned.
perturbation.justification: The original text required the recipient to maintain confidentiality with at least the same standard of care they use to protect their own confidential information. The changed text mandates 'no less care than a reasonable person would exercise under similar circumstances'. This introduces ambiguity, as a 'reasonable person' standard may be lower than the recipient's actual standard for their own highly sensitive information. This creates a contradiction because the recipient could now argue that using a 'reasonable person' standard fulfills their obligation, even if it's less than the care they use for their own information, directly conflicting with the original intention and contradicting the Confidential Information Obligations outlined in the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: The parties submit to the non -exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of that State.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of that State for disputes under $100,000 AUD, and to arbitration for disputes exceeding that amount.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the jurisdiction from non-exclusive to exclusive for smaller disputes, and then introducing arbitration for larger disputes creates a direct conflict. The original agreement allows parties to pursue legal action in other jurisdictions, but the modification restricts it. This is in direct contradiction with clause 6's initial statement that 'This agreement is governed by the laws of New South Wales, Australia', as it is unclear which legal path the parties need to take.
perturbation.contradicted_text: GENERAL The parties make no warranties or representations to each other in relation to any Confidential Information including (without limitation) as to the accur acy or completeness of any Confidential Information. A power or right in this agreement may only be waived in writing, signed by the party to be bound by the waiver, and this agreement may only be amended or supplemented in writing executed by both parties . The obligations in this agreement survive the termination or purported termination of this agreement. This agreement is governed by the laws of New South Wales, Australia. The parties submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of that State for disputes under $100,000 AUD, and to arbitration for disputes exceeding that amount.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the jurisdiction clause from non-exclusive to exclusive for disputes under $100,000 AUD and introduces arbitration for larger disputes. This directly contradicts the original agreement which allows parties to pursue legal action in other jurisdictions. The contradicted text contains the statement 'This agreement is governed by the laws of New South Wales, Australia'. The introduction of exclusive jurisdiction for smaller disputes and arbitration for larger disputes conflicts with the initial statement regarding the agreement being governed by the laws of New South Wales, Australia, creating a direct conflict and uncertainty about the legal path parties need to take. The parties now can't resolve all issues under New South Wales, Australia law, so there is a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: For this purpose (“Purpose ”), the parties intend to make confidential information available to each other , and wish to protect this information in accordance with this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: For this purpose (“Purpose ”), the parties may make confidential information available to each other , and wish to protect this information in accordance with this agreement, if they see fit.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'intend to make' to 'may make... if they see fit' changes the obligation of disclosing parties. The original text establishes that disclosing parties will in fact share information. However, the changed clause makes it discretionary. It therefore introduces a direct internal contradiction if other clauses rely on that Confidential Information being exchanged such as in clause 4 about Confidential Information Obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION OBLIGATIONS In consideration of each party disclosing its Confidential Information to the other party for the Purpose, the Recipient must ensure that Confidential Information is:  maintained confidential with at least the standard of care that the Recipient maintains t o protect its own confidential information;  not disclosed to any Unauthorised Person;  only used for the Purpose; and  not reproduced in any form except as required for the purpose. The Recipient must ensure that access to Confidential Information is only given to those of its officers, employees and advisers who require access for the Purpose, and that those officers, employees and advisers are informed of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information an d kee p that information confidential. Parties to whom the information is imparted must be warned that include d ‘insider information ,’ regarding publicly listed and traded shares may preclud e them from such trade in those shares . The Confidential Informatio n remains at all times the property of the Discloser, and other than as provided in this agreement, no rights in respect of the Confidential Information are granted or conveyed to the Recipient. Confidentiality & Non -Disclosure Agreement ICT Strategic Consulting 2 of 2 Ph: +61 (02) 9591 1464 56 Minnamorra Avenue Earlwood NSW 2206 Australia www.ICTStrategicConsulting.com In the event that the Recipient is legally required to disclo se any Confidential Information, the Recipient must immediately notify the Discloser of that fact. Within 7 days of a written request by the Discloser, the Recipient must return all Confidential Information, and provide a signed certificate stating that al l Confidential Information in the Recipient's possession or control has been returned.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the parties 'intend to make confidential information available to each other,' implying an obligation to share this information. The changed text alters this to 'may make... if they see fit,' making the sharing of confidential information discretionary. This directly contradicts the 'CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION OBLIGATIONS' section, which begins with 'In consideration of each party disclosing its Confidential Information to the other party for the Purpose...' The changed text introduces an ambiguity that undermines the entire basis for the confidentiality obligations outlined in the contradicted text, as there is no longer a guaranteed reciprocal disclosure of information. The change breaks compatibility with the contradicted section, making enforcement unclear and creating a logical inconsistency.
